---
title: "08 · Continuous Distributions and the Normal"
subtitle: "From PMFs to PDFs — and the bell curve at the centre of statistics"
lesson-id: "t5.continuous-distributions"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t3.discrete-distributions", "t4.definite-integral"]
unlocks: ["t5.variance-clt"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "databases"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T3 L15 — Discrete Distributions](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)
   and [T4 L08 — Definite Integral](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with PMFs $P(X = k)$ and expected values?
3. Recall $\int e^{-x^2} dx$ is the Gaussian (T5 L06 S1)?
:::

## Touch

A **continuous random variable** has a **probability density function
(PDF)** $f(x)$. Probabilities are **areas** under the PDF.

```{ojs}
viewof distSel = Inputs.select(["uniform on [0, 1]", "exponential, λ=1", "standard normal N(0, 1)"], {value: "standard normal N(0, 1)", label: "Distribution:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(distSel) {
    case "uniform on [0, 1]": info = "f(x) = 1 for x ∈ [0, 1], else 0. ∫₀¹ 1 dx = 1 ✓. P(0.2 < X < 0.5) = 0.3."; break;
    case "exponential, λ=1": info = "f(x) = e^(−x) for x ≥ 0. ∫₀^∞ e^(−x) dx = 1 ✓. P(X > 1) = e^(−1) ≈ 0.37."; break;
    case "standard normal N(0, 1)": info = "f(x) = (1/√(2π)) · e^(−x²/2). Bell curve. ∫₋∞^∞ f dx = 1 (T5 L06 Gaussian)."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

For continuous variables, $P(X = a) = 0$ — only intervals have
positive probability: $P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx$.

## Wonder

1. Why is $P(X = a) = 0$ for any single point in continuous case?
2. Total area under a PDF must equal 1. Why?
3. Why does the **normal distribution** appear so often in nature?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **uniform** distribution on $[a, b]$ has density $f(x) = 1/(b - a)$
for $x \in [a, b]$, else 0. Verify $\int f = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_a^b 1/(b-a) \, dx = (b - a) / (b - a) = 1$ ✓.

Uniform models "pick a random number in $[a, b]$ with equal
probability for any subinterval of equal length."
</details>

### Problem 2

The **expected value** of a continuous random variable is
$E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx$.

Compute $E[X]$ for $X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 1]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$E[X] = \int_0^1 x \cdot 1 \, dx = 1/2$.

Symmetry: the average of a uniform random variable is the midpoint.
</details>

### Problem 3

The **standard normal** $N(0, 1)$ has PDF
$$\phi(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-x^2/2}.$$
Show that $\int \phi = 1$ using the Gaussian integral $\int e^{-u^2}
du = \sqrt{\pi}$ (T5 L06 S1).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute $u = x/\sqrt{2}$, $du = dx/\sqrt{2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int \phi(x) \, dx = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int e^{-x^2/2} \, dx
= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \int e^{-u^2} \, du
= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = 1$ ✓.

The $\sqrt{2\pi}$ in the formula is exactly what makes the area
equal to 1.
</details>

### Problem 4

The **general normal** $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ has PDF
$$f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x - \mu)^2 / (2 \sigma^2)}.$$

What is $E[X]$? (Hint: by symmetry around $\mu$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$E[X] = \mu$. The PDF is symmetric around $\mu$, so the *centre*
(mean) is $\mu$.

Standard deviation = $\sigma$ (Lesson 09).
</details>

### Problem 5

The **exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda > 0$: $f(x) =
\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \ge 0$. Verify $\int f = 1$ and
compute $E[X]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_0^\infty \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = [-e^{-\lambda x}]_0^\infty
= 1$ ✓.

$E[X] = \int_0^\infty x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = 1/\lambda$
(by integration by parts).

The exponential models *waiting times*: time until the next email,
the next radioactive decay, the next earthquake. $E[X] = 1/\lambda$
is the average wait.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **continuous random variable** $X$ has a **PDF** $f(x)$ such that
$$P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x) \, dx.$$

**Properties:**

- $f(x) \ge 0$ for all $x$.
- $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x) \, dx = 1$.
- $P(X = a) = 0$ for any single point.

**Cumulative distribution function (CDF):** $F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f \, dt$.

**Expected value:** $E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx$.

**Common continuous distributions:**

| Distribution | PDF | Mean |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform on $[a, b]$ | $1/(b-a)$ | $(a + b)/2$ |
| Exponential rate $\lambda$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}, x \ge 0$ | $1/\lambda$ |
| Normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\mu$ |

The **normal distribution** is uniquely characterised by being the
limit of sums of many independent random variables (Central Limit
Theorem, Lesson 09).

:::

> *Continuous random variables: probabilities are integrals of PDFs;*
> *Total area = 1; single points have zero probability;*
> *Normal $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is the central distribution of statistics.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> $X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 4]$. Compute $P(1 \le X \le 3)$ and
> $E[X]$.

PDF: $f(x) = 1/4$ on $[0, 4]$.

$P(1 \le X \le 3) = \int_1^3 1/4 \, dx = 2/4 = 1/2$.

$E[X] = (0 + 4)/2 = 2$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $f(x) = 2x$ on $[0, 1]$. Is this a PDF?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_0^1 2x \, dx = 1$ ✓ and $f \ge 0$ ✓. **Yes.**
</details>

**W2.** $E[X]$ for the PDF in W1?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_0^1 x \cdot 2x \, dx = \int 2x^2 \, dx = 2/3$.
</details>

**W3.** $X \sim N(5, 4)$ — what is $\mu, \sigma$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mu = 5, \sigma = 2$ (since $\sigma^2 = 4$).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** $X \sim \text{Exp}(2)$. $P(X > 1)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(X > 1) = \int_1^\infty 2 e^{-2x} dx = e^{-2} \approx 0.135$.
</details>

**P2.** Find $c$ so $f(x) = c x^2$ is a PDF on $[0, 1]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_0^1 c x^2 dx = c/3 = 1 \Rightarrow c = 3$.
</details>

**P3.** $X \sim N(0, 1)$. Approximate $P(-1 \le X \le 1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Standard normal table or 68-95-99.7 rule: about 68% within 1 SD.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\approx 0.6827$** (the famous "68% within 1 SD" rule).

The empirical rule: $\approx 68\%$ within $1\sigma$, $\approx 95\%$
within $2\sigma$, $\approx 99.7\%$ within $3\sigma$ — used widely in
quality control and statistical reasoning.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why does the normal appear in so many contexts?** Hint: the
**Central Limit Theorem** (Lesson 09) says the sum (or average) of
many independent random variables, regardless of their original
distribution, tends to a normal distribution.

What does this say about real-world phenomena like heights, test
scores, measurement errors?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**These quantities are *sums* of many small independent influences.**
Heights = many genetic + environmental contributions. Test scores =
sum of correctness on many small questions. Measurement errors =
accumulation of many tiny noise sources.

CLT says: regardless of the distribution of each contribution,
their *sum* is approximately normal.

This is the **single most important fact in statistics**, and the
reason the normal distribution dominates statistical practice.
Without CLT, we'd need a different distribution for every situation.
</details>

**S2.** **In ML: weight initialisation.** Neural network weights are
typically initialised from a normal distribution (or its variant). Why
not uniform?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Two reasons.** (1) Activations through deep networks involve sums
of many weighted inputs — by CLT, these sums are approximately
normal regardless of the input distribution. Initialising weights to
match this *intermediate* statistics (e.g., **Xavier/Glorot
init**) keeps the activation magnitudes stable through the depth.

(2) Symmetry breaking: random non-zero values prevent every neuron
in a layer from learning the same thing.

Specific schemes: Xavier (variance $2/(n_{in} + n_{out})$), He
(variance $2/n_{in}$ for ReLU). All variants of normal initialisation
designed to keep gradients from vanishing or exploding through depth.

The continuous normal distribution is the foundational building block
of *every* well-trained neural network.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$P(X = 5)$ is small but non-zero."** For continuous $X$, it's
   *exactly zero*. Only intervals have positive probability.
2. **"PDF values are probabilities."** No — they're *densities*.
   $f(x) = 100$ doesn't mean "100% chance"; it means high density at
   $x$. Probabilities are areas, not heights.
3. **"Normal = N(0, 1) only."** $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ has parameters.
   $N(0, 1)$ is the *standard* normal; others scale and shift.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the property $\int f = 1$ for any PDF?
2. Without scrolling back, what's the mean of $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $1/\lambda$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[09 · Variance & CLT](../09-variance-clt/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (weight initialisation).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T3 L15 — Discrete Distributions](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)** —
  PMF generalises to PDF.
- **[T4 L08 — Definite Integral](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)** —
  computes probabilities and expected values.

### What this unlocks

- **[09 · Variance & CLT](../09-variance-clt/index.qmd)** — the
  spread, and the universality of the normal.
- **[10 · Statistical Inference](../10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)**

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Weight initialisation (S2). Gaussian
processes (Bayesian models). Variational autoencoders (Gaussian
latent space). Diffusion models (Gaussian noise → image).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Statistics & data science.** The normal is the workhorse of
modeling, hypothesis testing, confidence intervals.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics.** Gaussians describe quantum wavefunctions of bound
particles, equilibrium thermal distributions, measurement
uncertainties.
:::
